coredata.dc:title: Detection of sialic acid using boronic-acid-functionalized metal organic framework UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Huazheng
Li, Yanhong
Huang, Aisheng
coredata.dc:description: Sialic acid (SA) is a crucial component of glycoproteins and glycolipids on the cellular membrane, which is essential for maintaining the function of cell membranes, such as cell recognition and communication. Simultaneously, sialic acid plays a significant role in many physiological and pathological processes. Hence, it is urgent to develop a simple and sensitive strategy for determining sialic acid. In this work, a new metal-organic framework called UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 has been designed and synthesized for the recognition and detection of sialic acid. The boronic acid functional group in UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 can bind to a diol moiety of the glycerol side chain of sialic acid, which will attenuate or even quench the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2, thus opening a new road to detect sialic acid. Based on the measurement results, sialic acid can be quantitatively measured in a linear range of 0.05–2.5 mmoL/L with the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe. The detection limit of sialic acid is as low as 0.025 mmol/L. Furthermore, the boronic-acid functionalized probe UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 displays high sensitivity and high selectivity to recognize the sialic acid in mouse serum samples. Therefore, the developed UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 can be used as a promising probe to identify and detect sialic acid in the practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Fluorescent probe
UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2
Sialic acid
Fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recognition and detection of sialic acid with UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2
Determination of sialic acid in mouse serum
Synthesis and characterizations of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2
Determination of sialic acid in mouse serum samples

All members in Prof. Y. Tian’ group of ECNU are thanked for kindly help in fluorescence sensing experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the recognition of sialic acid (SA) by using functional MOF UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2
(a) FESEM, (b) TEM, and (c, d, e, f) EDX elemental mapping of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectrum of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2.
XPS spectra of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2. (a) C 1s spectrum, (b) O 1s spectrum, (C) N 1s spectrum, (D) B 1s spectrum.

(a) Effects of interfering substances and SA on the fluorescence intensity of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2. (b) Interference study for SA detection. (c) Fluorescence spectra of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 solution mixed with different SA concentrations. (d) The linear calibration plot of F/F0.
Fluorescence spectra of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 added with 200 μL mouse serum (a), and with mixtures of 200 μL mouse serum and various SA concentrations, respectively (b).

head.ce:title: Detection of sialic acid using boronic-acid-functionalized metal organic framework UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Chemical Processes, Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University, Dongchuan Road 500, Shanghai, 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Chemical Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Chemical Processes, Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University, Dongchuan Road 500, Shanghai, 200241, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sialic acid (SA) is a crucial component of glycoproteins and glycolipids on the cellular membrane, which is essential for maintaining the function of cell membranes, such as cell recognition and communication. Simultaneously, sialic acid plays a significant role in many physiological and pathological processes. Hence, it is urgent to develop a simple and sensitive strategy for determining sialic acid. In this work, a new metal-organic framework called UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 has been designed and synthesized for the recognition and detection of sialic acid. The boronic acid functional group in UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 can bind to a diol moiety of the glycerol side chain of sialic acid, which will attenuate or even quench the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2, thus opening a new road to detect sialic acid. Based on the measurement results, sialic acid can be quantitatively measured in a linear range of 0.05–2.5 mmoL/L with the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe. The detection limit of sialic acid is as low as 0.025 mmol/L. Furthermore, the boronic-acid functionalized probe UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 displays high sensitivity and high selectivity to recognize the sialic acid in mouse serum samples. Therefore, the developed UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 can be used as a promising probe to identify and detect sialic acid in the practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Sialic acid (SA) has a 9-carbon monosaccharide molecular structure [1], which is mainly distributed in human serum [2,3]. Sialic acid is an important component of glycoproteins and glycolipids in the cell membrane [4]. Hence, sialic acid is vital to the permeability and integrity of the cell structure. Moreover, sialic acid plays a significant role in cell recognition [5], signal transmission [6], immunity [7], cancer metastasis [8], and protective effect [9,10]. For example, serum sialic acid is widely used as a common clinical biomarker [11] and also served as a biomarker in a persistent inflammatory response in cardiovascular disease [12]. Sialic-acid-guided therapies strategies can be proposed for the prevention and treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection [13]. However, the overexpression of sialic acid is associated with a variety of diseases including cardiovascular disease, leukemia, lung cancer and colon cancer [14,15]. Therefore, it is essential to detect the content of sialic acid.

A variety of methods have been explored to determine the content of sialic acid, including colorimetric sensor [16], chromatography [17], electrochemical method [18], enzymatic assay [19]. However, the colorimetric method is susceptible to interference for detection and the sensitivity is low. For chromatography method, the samples need to be pretreated and the preparation process is complex. The weakness of electrochemical methods is poor repeatability and time-consuming, while enzymatic assay is high cost and instability because enzyme is easily inactivated. These deficiencies limited these methods for further application. Recently, fluorescence methods have been drawn great attention on account of their high sensitivity, selectivity, and simplicity in technology.

So far, various materials, such as carbon dots [20], nanoparticles [21], are used as fluorescent probes. In particular, boronic acid can react with a diol moiety of the glycerol side chain through reversible covalent interactions [22,23]. Therefore, boronic acid derivatives can bind to a diol moiety of the glycerol side chain of sialic acid to identify and monitor sialic acid. However, the boronic acid functional groups to recognize sialic acid are usually prepared by post-modification, normally resulting in low sensitivity due to insufficiency of the amount of boronic acid functional groups. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a new probe with abundant boronic acid functional groups to measure sialic acid.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), composed of metal ions and organic ligands, have been widely applied for catalysis [24], gas storage [25], gas separation [26], drug delivery and release [27,28]. Due to diverse structure, highly ordered porosity, good biocompatibility [29–31], MOFs have also been applied for chemical sensors [32–35], but only a few were reported for monitoring sialic acid [36]. In this study, a MOF called UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 with boronic acid functional groups is successfully prepared as a fluorescent probe for measuring sialic acid. UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 is prepared with a one-step solvothermal method by using 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2) and 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid (CPB) as organic ligands (Fig. S1), introducing boronic acid functional groups into the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 framework. Among the organic ligands, the aromatic or conjugated π moieties are susceptible to be excited and emits luminescence upon irradiation [37,38]. Therefore, the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 belongs to luminescent MOFs (LMOFs). But when the boronic acid functional groups of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 bind to diol moieties of the glycerol side chain of sialic acid, the fluorescence intensity of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 will decrease or even quench (Fig. 1 ). Hence, the proposed UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 will exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity to identify and detect sialic acid according to the reduction of fluorescence intensity.

N-acetylneuraminic acid (Neu5Ac, 98%, Macklin), calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2, 99%, Macklin), 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES, 99%, Macklin), zirconium (IV) chloride (ZrCl4, 99.5%, Aladdin), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%, Aladdin), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2, 99%, Aladdin), ethanol (99.5%, Aladdin), D-(+)-glucose monohydrate (99.5%, Aladdin), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%, Sinopharm), acetic acid (HAc, 99.5%, Sinopharm), 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid (CPB, 98%, Bide Pharmatech), d-fructose (Ruji Biotechnology), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9%, Aladdin), d-mannose (Ruji Biotechnology), D-(+)-galactose (Ruji Biotechnology), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99%, Titan), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%, Titan), urea (99%, Titan), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 99.5%, Titan), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%, Titan).

UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 was obtained by simply solvothermal synthesis method based on the previously reported processes [39]. Typically, 0.64 g ZrCl4 was added into 50 mL N, N-dimethylformamide to get a clear solution under ultrasonication. Then 0.20 g concentrated HCl and 14.00 g HAc were added in sequence into the solution. 0.12 g BDC-NH2 and 0.34 g CPB were put into another 50 mL DMF and stirred to obtain a homogeneous solution. Hereafter, the two solutions were mixed and then keeping stirring for 30 min. At last, the mixture solution was transferred in a sealer and reacted at 120 °C for one day. After synthesis, the solution was cooled down and centrifuged. After centrifugation, the obtained precipitate was immersed in DMF and ethanol for 3 days, respectively. Finally, the light yellow powder, named UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2, was collected by centrifuging and then drying at 100 °C for two days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition and detection of sialic acid with UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2
ce:section.ce:para: To test the selectivity of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 over sialic acid, interference testing was carried out. Herein, d-fructose, CaCl2, d-mannose, NaCl, D-(+)-galactose, KCl, d-mannose, Na2SO4, urea, NH4Cl, and sialic acid (Neu5Ac) were diluted to the same concentration with HEPES buffer solution. UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 powder was added into the HEPES buffer solution to prepare 0.2 mg/mL UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 solution. The same volume of the interference and sialic acid solution was droped into 2 mL UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 solution, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of the mixture were collected. Moreover, 0.2 mL different interference solution was also transferred into UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 solution which contained 0.2 mL sialic acid solution. The fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded with fluorescence spectrophotometer. Furthermore, the fluorescence experiments of 0.2 mg/mL UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 solution mixed with different concentration sialic acid solution were also investigated. An excitation wavelength of 320 nm was used in all the fluorescence experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sialic acid in mouse serum
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical application of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2, mouse serum was selected as the analyte. The mouse serum was added with pH = 7.4 HEPES buffer, and then centrifuged to get the supernatant. The supernatant was treated with a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain the clear solution. Then, the clear solution was added with concentrated sulfuric acid, and incubated for 1 h at 80 °C. After cooling down, the mixed solution was centrifuged to obtain the supernatant. Subsequently, NaOH solution was added into the supernatant to neutralize acid. Finally, the treated mouse serum was measured to get total sialic acid content. In the recovery experiments, the mouse serum samples were diluted with HEPES buffer (pH = 7.4) for detection before testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2
ce:section.ce:para: UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 is a mixed-ligand MOF by using BDC-NH2 and CPB as organic ligands (Fig. S1). As displayed in Fig. 2 a and b, the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 particles are of an octahedral shape and have crystal sizes of approximate 300–500 nm, which is the similar to that of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S2a). In Fig. 3 a, all peaks of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 is consistent with that of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S2b) in the XRD pattern [40], suggesting that CPB can be easily incorporated into the UiO-66-NH2 framework without destroying the crystallographic structure of UiO-66-NH2, because the CPB structure is very similar to BDC-NH2, such as a rod-like ligand structure, ligand lengths, carboxyl groups used for coordination. Therefore, CPB will provide the desired functional group boronic acid into the UiO-66-NH2 framework [39]. The successful preparation of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 was also proved by FT-IR characterization (Fig. 3b). The peak in 3357 cm−1 is attributed to bond vibration of the -NH- group [41], and the peaks of 1654 and 1576 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CO of carboxyl [42]. Furthermore, the peaks of the stretching vibration of C-N group are observed at 1436 cm−1. 1388 and 1257 cm−1 can correspond to B-O-H bending vibration [4]. Results reveal that CPB is successfully introduced into the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 by one-step solvothermal method. EDX studies show that C, O, N and B elements are uniformly distributed over the crystal (Fig. 2c, d, e, f), indicating that boronic acid groups have introduced into the structure of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2.

To further confirm the successful introduction of boronic acid groups into the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2, XPS analysis of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 was performed. The XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4 a) shows five main peaks including 283.9 eV, 284.5 eV, 285.2 eV, 288.2 eV and 289.0 eV, which are ascribed to C-B, C-C/CC, C-O, C-N and C-CC [43,44], respectively. In Fig. 4b, CO (531.15 eV) and C-O (532.48 eV) are shown in the O 1s spectrum [45]. The peaks presented at 400.6 and 402.3 eV in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) are attributed to the N-C and N-H groups [46], respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the B 1s spectrum of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 indicates the presence of B-C (190.4 eV), B-O (192.4 eV) [47]. All the above results demonstrate that UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 with boronic acid functional groups is successfully synthesized. The material stability has great influence on the practical application for a fluorescent probe. Hence, the stability of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 in buffer solvent is also investigated. In Fig. S3, the all peaks of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 keep unchanged in the buffer solvent (pH = 5.5–7.4) after soaked for 7 days. The results indicate UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 is very stable in the HEPES buffer solution.

In order to prove that the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe has good selectivity to sialic acid, interference assays are needed to evaluate. The related components commonly found in the serum are applied to study the effect on fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2. As shown in Fig. 5 a, fructose, urea, glucose, NH4 +, mannose, Na+, galactose, serine, threonine, Ca2+, Cl−, SO4 2− and K+ have no remarkable effect on the reduction of the fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 at the same concentration. Hence, the ratio of F/F0 is almost equal to 1 (F0 and F are the intensity value of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 before and after the presence of interfering components or sialic acid). On the contrary, the fluorescence intensity of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe obviously decreases when sialic acid is added. Moreover, when sialic acid and interfering components are present together in the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 solution, the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 is less than or equal to one in Fig. 5b, suggesting that UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 can well recognize and detect sialic acid in the presence of other interferences. The experimental results demonstrate the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe has excellent selectivity to sialic acid and immunity from various interference substances.

In Fig. 5c, the fluorescence of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 is gradually decreased along with increasing the concentration of sialic acid. For the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2, boronic acid groups contribute to capture photoinduced electrons and holes. When the sialic acid is added, boronic acid molecules can combine with a diol moiety of the glycerol side chain of sialic acid and then form a five-membered heterocyclic ring, resulting in the reduction of the fluorescence intensity of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 (Fig. 1). Based on the testing results, a relationship of its fluorescence intensity with sialic acid concentration in the linear range of 0.05–2.5 mmol/L can be established as a function of F/F0 = 0.99–0.11x (R2 = 0.98) (Fig. 5d), suggesting that sialic acid can be quantitatively detected by using UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.025 mmol/L (LOD = 3δ/S, S is the slope of the fitting curves and δ is the standard deviation, which is many times replicating fluorescence determinations of blank solutions). Table S2 shows the comparison of the LOD of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe with other fluorescence probes reported in literatures. It can be seen that the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe has a higher sensitivity for the detection of sialic acid. The lifetime of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 has no any change with the addition of sialic acid (Fig. S4). Additionally, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is collected to examine the optical properties of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe. Obviously, the absorbance of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 apparently increases after the addition of sialic acid (Fig. S5). All the experimental results imply that sialic acid reacts with UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2, leading to decrease fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of sialic acid in mouse serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of assessing the potential applications of the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe, sialic acid in mouse serum was measured. The total levels of sialic acid in mouse serum are in the range from 3.18 to 4.22 mmol/L according to the previous report [48]. The testing concentration of total sialic acid (TSA) in mouse serum samples is about 3.6 mmol/L by using the as-prepared UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe (Fig. 6 a and b), which is in the range of 3.18–4.22 mmol/L. Additionally, the relative recovery rate (%) was also measured. Briefly, the recovery rate (%) was the ratio of the testing concentration value to the actual concentration value of sialic acid added in this experiment. In Table S1, the recovery rate ranges from 94 to 100%, indicating the high reproducibility of the proposed fluorescent UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe, which is helpful for practical applications in clinical diagnosis of sialic acid related diseases.

In summary, a new MOF UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 functionalized with boronic acid is prepared and acted as a new fluorescence sensor by one-step solvothermal method. The UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 can be applied for the quantitative determination of sialic acid in the linear range from 0.05 to 2.5 mmoL/L. The UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 probe possesses many merits such as excellent selectivity, reliability, good stability. In particular, this probe can be used to measure the concentration of total sialic acid in mouse serum samples with satisfactory results. Because of the good performance, the UiO-66-NH2@B(OH)2 can be developed for practical applications in clinical diagnosis of sialic acid -related diseases.

Huazheng Yu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, and analysis, Writing-Reviewing and Editing; Yanhong Li: Writing-Reviewing and Editing; Aisheng Huang: Writing-Reviewing and Editing, Supervision. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21878100, 22075084), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (40500-20101222093) are acknowledged. All members in Prof. Y. Tian’ group of ECNU are thanked for kindly help in fluorescence sensing experiments.
